content: When you look at Rust's formatting system via the fmt trait, you will quickly realize that hex and octal output is rarely used for display to humans. Instead, it serves as a low-level debugging mechanism or a way to generate machine-readable configuration files where exact bit patterns matter. The core distinction lies in how we implement these traits: the Binary format uses {:b} with base 2, Octal uses {:o} with base 8, and Hex formats use either LowerHex ({:x}, lowercase x) or UpperHex ({:X}, uppercase X). Beyond just the character set, you must also understand the # flag which forces a zero-prefix to be printed before the number. For instance, if you want to print an integer u32 as hexadecimal with the 0x prefix, you simply use format!("{:#x}", val), whereas without the hash it omits that prefix and might result in leading zeros depending on the implementation details of the specific target system or library crate. When choosing between LowerHex and UpperHex, the convention is to default to lowercase x unless your documentation explicitly requires uppercase X for compatibility with legacy tools or specific schema validators; this matters because many parsers are strict about case sensitivity when reading raw binary dumps from Rust code. Similarly, Octal formatting via {:o} is often a historical artifact in C-style languages but in modern Rust it is primarily useful when you need to represent byte values without hex expansion, though the # flag here behaves differently than in Hex by enforcing an octal prefix 0 instead of just leading zeros. The LowerExp and UpperExp traits with specs like {:e} are fundamentally different because they do not use a radix base at all; instead, they implement scientific notation where you must manually parse the exponent part since there is no standard hex digit mapping for powers of ten in the same way as base 2 or base 16. The most critical decision point for an engineer is whether to write your own formatter using the Write trait with a custom loop that calculates digits and appends characters, or to leverage the built-in Display trait which handles all these alternate radix traits internally through specialized code paths in the standard library. If you are building a high-performance binary serialization crate where every cycle counts, you should avoid calling into fmt::Binary directly because it involves string allocation and parsing overhead; instead, implement custom Write implementations that iterate over bits or nibbles using bitwise operations to construct the buffer without ever invoking the formatter itself. For general application code where readability matters more than raw throughput, stick with the built-in traits but be mindful that {:#x} can produce leading zeros which might break strict schema validation if your downstream tooling expects fixed-width fields; in those specific cases, you must strip the prefix manually or use the Write trait to implement a custom formatter that conditionally adds the 0x only when necessary. The # flag is also essential for distinguishing between signed and unsigned representations in some contexts, but note that Rust's fmt::Binary does not support signed hex output by default because negative numbers require two's complement representation which complicates simple character emission; you must implement your own logic to handle sign bits if you need to format signed integers as hexadecimal. Finally, remember that these traits are all part of the std::fmt module and rely on the same underlying Write trait mechanics, so when debugging a formatting issue involving {:b} or {:X}, check whether your type implements the necessary trait correctly before assuming it is a bug in the standard library implementation.
